transcript.text: Hello.
Hello.
I hope you don't mind.
I have headphones on.
No, no.
That's all good.
No worries.
Hi.
How are you?
Just fine.
Yourself?
I'm doing good.
Thank you.
Good.
Good.
Yeah.
Oh, man.
Okay.
Yes.
Hello.
Hello.
Nice talking to you.
Nice to meet you.
Yeah.
Nice to meet you.
Yeah.
I'm happy to go through this.
I can answer some questions for you.
I'll ask you some questions.
I'm happy to chop it up a little bit.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
name is Matthew.
I have a background in editorial.
I was the editor-in-chief of TechCrunch for about 10 years, so I ran editorial.
saw.
Yeah.
That was interesting.
It was fun.
It's intense.
Intense 10 years.
Basically through the entire kind of like Zerp era startup boom.
So that was fun.

So yeah, editorial operations there, news operations, as well as operations for Disrupt, our series of events throughout the world, programming and editorial there, and then also startup battlefield or startup competition, along with a bunch of other products.
It's like newsletters and morning shows and Netflix pilots and everything under the sun.
So I came out of there, went into startups, spent a year as a VP of marketing and startup.
And then now I'm here as chief content officer.
Got talking with Daniel and Marcel and got really excited about their vision for the way that they were sort of atomizing the process by which content is created.
I had thought about that.
I had thought about content that way.

And unfortunately, just been a little under equipped to be able to execute on that from a product perspective, because media companies, especially news organizations are just usually hopping from lily pad to lily pad trying to survive, get the news out on deadline, etc.
So it's very interesting to see that they were thinking of it in a similar way.

And so my job here is to help both articulate the process by which excellent quality content is created, but then also to help judge whether or not or evaluate whether or not we are actually meeting that mark, and then work backwards from there into how to develop the processes and all of that that get us there.
So that's why I'm here.

And I work closely with the delivery team, which is our managing editors and content managers and directors and that sort of thing to deliver client work, evaluate that work and ensure that we're doing the best we can do with our internal tooling.
So that's a snapshot of my job here.
Just want to give you a little bit of context there.
And then happy to hear a little bit about you and what you've been up to.
Yeah.
Hi, Matthew.
My name's Sheba, of course.
So I want to talk maybe like how I arrived here.
I've always been a writer.
Right out of college, I worked basically in startups.
I did some magazine work, like in the era of magazines.
I worked at Time, did some work at Essence.
I've always loved the written word, I would say.
But there was an experience that really shaped me, which was so out of college, I worked at a company called Brand Yourself.
They were more of like a, they were on Shark Tank.
They got all this money and they had built this like reputation management software.
So at that job, I was like an account manager, like content strategist, if you will.
And I was overseeing client accounts and writing and building up their social channels.
So they would rank positively.
To push down negative information that would show up in search results.
So that was a really cool experience for me because I got to mix like editorial, project management, account work together.
I ended up leaving Brand Yourself because I got a job at a company called Story Hunter.
The company goes by Glimmer now.
So Story Hunter was kind of like, they were still a very small team.
They were a group of journalists who had worked at Current TV, who built a platform that could connect media publishers with fixers, videographers across the world.
So I did some community management, editorial, like social work while I was there.
And then the work I did there really shaped how I thought about the future of editorial and the future of news.
I was also reporting on the side while doing all of these jobs because I love culture.
I love travel.
So I ended up applying to a grad program at NYU.
It's called Studio 20.
And Studio 20...
Plenty is not a traditional journalism program.
It kind of teaches journalists to be UX product people, right?
Like we were living sort of like the age of newspapers and tablets and people are getting their news on WhatsApp and like apps.
So the program kind of was designed to meet those needs.
And so we, as students, we would work, we would take courses in legacy media.
And do a lot of product work.
So through that program, I did some work at the Wall Street Journal on a lot of their newsletter products while I was there.
Ended up getting a fellowship at the FJP at the time.
That's called the Facebook Journalism Project.
This was in the era of, you know, all of the misinformation that contributed to one election in the United States.
And so, so I basically was in this world of like, I.
I had done this editorial work, and then I was a product person.
And where do those two things meet?
I really couldn't find that, right?
So after my time at Meta, they really liked me there.
They invited me to basically come into the company as a content designer, like content strategist, with their civic connection social impact team.
So I took that job, and I basically learned content strategy work, working closely with researchers, engineers, product designers on end-to-end content.
Long-form and short-form at Meta.

And then I kind of grew a little bit, like I needed more of a challenge while I was there, because the thing about big media, large tech companies is that you don't get to have your hands in as much work as you would like to.
And I'm coming from a background where like most of my work was in startups.
Like I love startups, I love growing companies, companies that are still figuring themselves out.
I feel like I thrive better in those spaces.
Where things are like moving pretty fast paced.
So I was approached by another company called Launch AI on LinkedIn.
And they were building a content engine.
And they're like, we don't know what this means, but we want to figure it out.
Like you have really great experience.
Like, how do you feel about coming to join us as a lead content engineer, like slash content designer at the company?
So my last role was pretty much that.
You know, I spent almost two years basically integrating artificial intelligence into content engine, content generation.
And basically at Launch AI, the business primarily first served real estate clients.
And then as they scaled, they were being approached for other different business needs.
So when I first arrived there, I was doing all of the messaging just like by myself.
And then as more people approach the business.
Really had to figure out how to use AI to create custom solutions.
that was also my task there.
In addition to the writing, I was designing a content engine, working closely with the product team and the engineering team.
And I also managed a team of content managers who were the human in the loop part of the process.
So that's really a very condensed version of how I arrived here.
I'm super excited about this company, GrowthX, because it really feels like something that I've been prepped for my entire career as far as a role.
Even reading about it, was like, this is not real.
This doesn't exist.

But yeah, I'm really curious to hear about what your expectations are for the person who will be filling this position and what you see as like a successful accomplishment of the role and how your management style of your teams are.
Yeah, sounds good.
Good.
Yeah, I can answer those questions for you.
We'll talk a little bit about that and then I have a couple for you.
The role internally is, I would guess, 40% content related, 16% work related.
And that number changes day to day because we are a startup, right?
And so on a day where the workflows are not workflowing, it's more content related, right?
It's like, okay, let's make sure that client work is getting shipped and we're keeping the ball rolling.
So there is always that expectation, of course, that externally, it's very duck's feet, like all startups.
So externally, the client is getting their deliverables.
They're happy with quality.
They feel taken care of and they feel like they're, you know, for progress towards their goals are being made.
That's great.

Internally, there is like, there's an internal facing surface of this that wouldn't exist at a typical agency, which is that we are building a product that we are using internally to deliver the client work.
And a good chunk of the work is basically product development.

It is articulating where the product is working properly and where it is not, understanding the why, you know, the inputs and the outputs of any individual workflow in a pipeline or the pipeline itself, and then being able to understand and articulate where the process is breaking down, because without articulation, you cannot then fix it, build the tool that are needed.

I think that what we found is that people that are great at content work and that have a deep content experience, they come in, they are more than capable, of course, of judging good content, and they're very skilled as writers and editors, but they fall down in the articulation of processes.
And that is, that's the big thing where it's like, if you're going to come, be prepared to explain precisely, give it to the best of your knowledge, what the way this thing is working.
And what the way it should work and what the delta is and try to define as much as possible, you know, how to bridge that gap.

That articulation process and then in the interim ways to complete the task, utilizing whatever tools are at your disposal, whether it's one of the dozens of automation tooling subscriptions that we already have or something that you know might work for this scenario to come up with an interim, like basically prototype for a solution is also a big part of the task.

Because then we take those prototypes and we're like, how do we productize these, you know, let's work on them, which can be exciting to some people or it can be intimidating or off-putting to others, which is why I mention it.
Because that is a different, it's a related skill set to articulating the problem because, you know, if you know kind of like what needs to happen, then theoretically.
Threatically, you could take that next step and be, you know, self-actualized about it and go like, oh, okay, I think I can plug this workflow into this tool and then get this output.
Oh, yeah, that works.
Okay, cool.
So I've done this.
Now let me document that process of follow ticket, right?
So that we can get that thing built into the pipeline, into the product.
You know, it needs to exist.
We have a need, you know, and then, of course, it's up to the eng team and Daniel is the CTO to prioritize and we figure all that out.
But there is always going to be, at any startup, there's always going to be a delta between what you have promised that you're going to deliver and what you're actually capable of delivering.
And if that gets too narrow, it means you're moving too slow.
And if it gets too wide, of course, that you're over your skis.
But somewhere between there is a healthy place for a startup because you never want to feel on balance.
That usually means that you're not moving rapidly enough or you're not discovering new problems quickly enough or you're...
Frankly, just misinformed and people are about to turn because they're either getting what they want.
so they're like, forget it.
This delta is too broad between what they can actually deliver and what they have built.
Right.
So I think that's, that's where we live.
That's a large chunk of the day to day is understanding the way that the things are working now, where the way that they should be working and helping to articulate that.
And then, of course, the other side of that day to day is, OK, cool, we've got some deliverables coming out of the end of these pipelines that we've worked with.
How do we get those into shape to present to clients, keep things on track, stay well organized with client deliverables?
You may be working with five or six, eight clients in your team, maybe working with those.
Generally speaking right now, most people at the, like, ME layer are working with about four to five clients, but that we hope will scale as the pipelines get more efficient.
I personally am doing...
So sort of like an experimental process, taking clients out of our sprint process, which would be like, hi, how do we build the tooling to do whatever you want to do?
Like, can we even do this for you?
And so we do, we engage them in a sprint process for several weeks.
Usually it's about eight to 12 weeks.
And then they come out at the end of the sprint process with like, okay, cool.
We're starting to produce content for them.
We now want know what they want.

We have built a lot of the internal artifacts that inform the content, whether that's company context, what personas they're writing for, how they want us to write the voice and tone, what we call writing guidelines.
And like those core artifacts that sort of been built, we've roughed out the pipeline for them.
And we've started to deliver calibration content where they're like, oh, yeah, thumbs up, down, or I'm gonna make some edits and we're gonna get back, right?
And we've started to deliver that.
So I'm grabbing a handful of those clients right now.
And my I'm running three of them alone with the pipelines with not alone.
I have an assistant that works with me, but essentially alone with no MEs or CMs.
And it works fine.
Like, yes, it requires high agency and it requires like that ticket filing mentality and that prototyping mentality.
But I'm running three clients solo without any of the other layers that exist across the org.

And so our vision for the future is that most MEs will be able to run like a decent number of clients with well-articulated, well-working pipelines and a couple of CMs to help them keep the things rolling, know, keep the spice flowing through the pipelines, organizing things, getting things all into shape, that sort of deal.
So the day-to-day has to always involve some sort of content sensibility, but that's more of like what you have to have in your hip pocket.
Like you have to be able to look at something and go, this is not it, right?
This is missing the mark or whatever.
But then the other side of the process.
Let's articulate that, let's build the tooling, let's figure out what needs to be built, and let's get that rolling.
Does that make sense?
It seems like, yeah, it feels like a merge between content sensibility, user UX-like systems thinking, and something that's like product.

Because, and I say that because of this program I went through, like it really helps me think also like content itself is a product, and it's something that's iterable, and it exists on an operational pipeline that is also iterable, because, you know, sometimes most companies want to scale.
The goal is usually growth or scale.

So what are the systems, and what are the products that are being made, and how can those things scale, and how are we keeping track of those things to be scaled, which is like, what's a bottleneck in the process?
Let's make a note on it.
Like there are certain things I've also learned in terms of doing a lot of the ticketing work in my earlier, the early parts of my career.
Career with engineering and like seeing what people are putting in the tickets and like triaging tickets to end or like customer success, right?
So those things have also informed a little bit of how I treat content and like end-to-end system.
So, yeah, it sounds cool.
Like, I think I really understand what you're saying.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's good.
And I'm glad that you have had that experience.
It is people like yourself that we're looking for, which are harder to find.
You know, it's actually, it's not that, sadly, media is in disarray at the moment.
So it's not hard to find like really experienced editors and writers out there on the market that are like looking for something new.
And I love people like that.
I've worked with, you know, hundreds of them over the years and really enjoy them.
But it is not a guarantee that they have also the systems thinking and the articulation ability that we need.
Because as you have probably worked with, you know, great writers or you, you've done writing yourself, you, you.
know, like, there are amazing writers that cannot actually tell you how they do it.
You know, they're a clean copy.
It's really crisp.
come up with a logical flourish where you're like, ah, this is amazing, right?
Like, hey, what did you call?
Oh, I don't know.
You know, inputs came in, some stuff happened in here, and then outputs came out, right?
And so our job is here, right?
Like, that's what we're doing.
We're taking this thing and then pulling it apart and exploding it out into its components.
And then understanding how to productize and then improve and iterate on each of those steps.

So obviously, have, you our pipelines do break things down and sort of articulate each one of them out into something that we feel that we can then fine tune to either achieve, you know, goals or improve on the goals that we have.
So someone in your position would not necessarily need to be an engineer or anything like that, but you kind of need to understand what...
Gears interlock to make the machine work.
And then know either, okay, this gear needs oil or this gear needs to be replaced.
know, those kinds of things are important to us.
Not to overuse a bunch of mixed metaphors, but I think you get the idea.
Yeah.
Yes.
I guess like when I, considering our conversation now, the question I do have is if there are multiple clients, like what's the, how does the metrics work, you know, per team, per pod?
Or is it that there's a standard metric or does it change depending on the kind of content?
Is there a multiplicity of content?
So we have internal metrics and external ones, right?
The external ones would, the ones that are important to the client, think none of them would be shocking to you.
Things like traffic growth on the cohort of content that we are producing for the client, like branded versus non-branded content, keyword ranking on.
Cluster keywords that we are producing content for them.
it's like, are we making forward progress and helping you to rank better for these keywords?
Some of our clients, we are drilling down as far as conversion, but it just depends.
Like some of the clients that we have are more sophisticated and we're gaining more sophisticated ones all the time as we grow.
We're starting to attract bigger clients, obviously.
And so they will have operations where they have, you know, they're using something like Gong or another system to track, you know, conversions all the way from acquisition to conversion.
And we can integrate with those systems and keep an eye on like how our content is affecting those or at least imply, infer how our content is affecting those pipelines.
So some of our clients are conversion related, but anyhow, that cluster of...
Of metrics or measurements is all relatively standard when it comes to content production.
It's just a matter of what the statement of work was for the client to begin with.

So if you get a client that comes out of Sprint and is handed over to you and you're working with them now, the statement of work would be like, hey, we're producing so many pieces of content for you a month or a year or whatever, or whatever the deliverable is.
It may be just pieces of content.
It may be a project where we're building like a directory of terms, right, or a database of whatever, you know, like directories.
We often, we have done that work.
It's like, okay, directory of apps that use your APIs.
Okay, we can build apps and, you know, make sure that that's all that.
So it's not just blog posts all day long, right?
We do build a lot of different stuff, but the metrics will be established for that.
And the, usually are statements of work and...
Contracts are not written around explicit goals because content is not really easy to do that way.
Like, okay, how long does take?
It depends, right?
What is your brand?
What is your brand traffic that already exists?
What are your priorities?
And how is the internet going to change between now and six months from now?
Who knows?

But what we can promise is we're going to produce deliverables on spec and the expectation, of course, is that we're going to watch all of the metrics that are important to you that you talked about to us that are important to you.
And we'll watch those weekly, monthly basis, and then talk to you about it and intelligently sketch out future strategy based on the way that that's working.
Okay, it is working.
Great.
We're going to lean in here.
It isn't working.
Let's lean out there and lean in somewhere else.
Whatever.
You know, those calls.
So the external metrics are pretty straightforward.
The internal metrics are a little bit different because they are largely related to efficiency.
And right now, most of them are...
Okay, but not great.
And this is to be expected because we're a startup.
So the efficiencies need to be discovered.
We have seen light at the end of the tunnel.
There are definitely ways that some of the pipelines that are working that if all of them work that way, we would be way in the black in terms of efficiency rating.
And efficiency for us, it can be defined a few ways and we are experimenting with different ways.
But one example of a way is like, okay, how much content are we producing per employee?
Right?
Like how many pieces of output are we producing per, you know, how much are we spending to make this content?
How much are we, how much time are we spending to make the content?
That's another big one, right?
Okay, cool.
Hey, this client needs eight pieces of content per week.
How long does it take us to produce that to spec?
Right?
From start to finish.
Okay.
Okay, we're going to begin production for this, start the stopwatch.
Now I think these eight are ready for the client to look at.
Maybe they're going to give some feedback and all of that, but that's really a separate thing we're measuring, right?
But we think, according to our internal judgment, that this is of quality, this is ready to present to the client.
How much time does that take?
Right?
And how much, so what is that per piece on average?
What does it take to make something?
And all of those numbers should be going down, right?
Like, is the pipeline getting more efficient?
Are you getting better at it?
Are you getting more skilled at it, you know, as a content manager or as an ME to guide your team to do it?
Like, are you enabling them with more efficiency by fixing their problems, by kicking things out of their way, you know, by going like, oh, I see exactly what you mean.
Okay, cool.
Let's, let's get a ticket together on this.
Let's, let's fix this.
It's automatable, you know, this seems repeatable.
And like, honestly, it's anything we're doing more than twice a week.
Automate.
Exactly.
Yes.
On automation, like I don't want people doing things 15 times a week.
I want them doing it, you know, if it's one or two times a week.
Okay.
All right.
How many weeks in a row have you done that?
Three?
Okay.
So that's like 30 minutes per week.
So that's like, you know, $4,500.
We're not thinking about it to the dollar right now because we're willing to spend the money to figure it out.
But eventually, you know, that's what it is.
That's how you price client work.
Our pricing client work at the moment is like, hey, I don't know.
How about this?
Right?
You know, and it's good.
We're profitable, which is great.
And we have, you know, money flowing in.
have revenue, which is all lovely.
And the churn is going down because we're finding, we're validating our clients better.
Like, okay, we want clients that actually know what client, what content work feels like on a regular basis, you know, that sort of thing.
but those efficiencies are the things that we're looking at internally.
So we have the...
Okay.
Internal metrics, pre-street forward, internal metrics still being defined, but largely related to efficiency, because that's how we win long term.
Yeah.

Okay, we're able to build efficient processes, because then when we, once we open up the platform to the public, you know, and say like, hey, are you a company that's looking for a content engine, we have built one, they need to know, okay, what is the efficiency of this, you know, what is it going to take me, how many people does it take me to run this thing, you know, what is it, what is it like, and we want, of course, those numbers to look great for them, so that's what we're doing internally.
Right.

In an instance where there is, how do you maybe talk to like the managing editors, where there has to be like a management of expectations, you know, with clients, you know, you've mentioned, kind of meeting things, creating things to spec.
What if the client says, listen, I want more, I want you guys to be able to figure out A, C, and D, and let's say there isn't the existence of.
of the automation.
Let's say there isn't the capacity to work as quickly as they want.
Typically, how are managing editors expected to manage client expectations if they're not in alignment?
Yeah, there may be, especially in steady state clients, there may be more interaction between a managing editor and a client, but the client relationships are typically handled by the directors.
And so the directors manage expectations largely based on that.

But as I mentioned, if you get a client that's in a steady state, the ME may be running that pretty much the whole time because the director's not really needed unless there's a big strategic reset that needs to be done or whatever.
But I've definitely had conversations.
I think I'm going to have another one this week where it's like with a client, hey, you're asking for X.
What you want is really going to cost you a lot of money.
So let's talk about it, right?
Like, you know, want, right?
And we are delivering to what our original spec was and
There's, you know, it's all the normal stuff that happens in client work.
Scope creep.
Well, can you do this?
Oh, yeah, we can.
Okay, sure.
You know, but if you want this every week, we need to talk about how much you're paying, right?
And how much time this is.
Some other ones are like opportunities that you seek that come up, like active listening on client calls.
It's not just, I'm going to read off your deliverables.
You're chopping it up with them on a weekly basis about like, hey, how do you feel?
What's the vibe?
What does this look like to you?
Is this meeting your expectations?
But then also, what are some opportunities for the future?
Is it, do we take existing, let's call it, let's use the unit of time, right?
Is it would take some of the existing time that we're doing to create blog posts here?
And maybe we start working on some, another project like a glossary, you know, directory or a glossary or a, some sort of other link magnet or maybe a newsletter, you know, other things like that.
Where can we shift in a portion?
our labor to make sure that we're still attacking the keywords and growth goals that you have, but maybe that we're not getting stagnant, just producing blog post after blog post for you.
It keeps the client engaged.
keeps them really interested in what you're doing for the future.
Um, and therefore the lock-in, you know, they, they're not a service bureau that poops out these little, you know, that this way.
And they're like, Oh, I can replace them with anybody else that would do the same thing.
Right.
Um, then the other component of it though is, is upselling basically.
Oh, you exactly.
You know, what could we do more for you?
Okay.
We could do things like we can build you a newsletter written by your CEO.
We'll take like 50 things your CEO has written, parse them to understand how they write.

And then we will take that information and then create writing guidelines and then build a pipeline around it and then create a template for your newsletter, run it on Beehive for you, control the, you know, we'll take your, your mailing list.
Make sure it gets delivered properly.
Do all the copy generation and proofreading, maybe even some images for each newsletter.
That's a new product, right?
That's a new work stream.
And so then if you're interested in that, let's write up a new contract.
Let's see what that looks like.
And then, okay, cool.
So our engagement just went from 20K a month to 50K a month.
But now we're running a newsletter for you.
And, you know, there's conversations and opportunities like that that have come up all the time.
If you're actively listening on the calls, you know, if they're like, you know, I don't know what else we can do.
And they're like, let's talk.
Let's pull that apart.
You know, what else?
You know, and one of the fun things is that you do have an enormous amount of visibility, if you care to, across the company to everything that everybody's doing for other clients.
So outside of conflicts of interest, where we isolate one client from another, you do get a decent view into like the scope of work that we're doing for other clients.
And you can see the things that we're building or creating, and you're like, oh, they would love this.
You know, this is exactly what I think.
And then that's part of the conversation, so.
That's really interesting.
I like the, which, I mean, I think most people should have a newsletter, so.

Which is very interesting to me because I, you know, I don't, actually a lot of my editorial product work has been, I think newsletters would be like the second thing I focused a lot on to, I did some work at the journal.
There's this other newsletter product called Salty and, you know, done Metrograph in New York, like streaming companies.
They, you're more likely to get the engagement you want from a newsletter before anything else.
This is my own personal belief, right?
But I think people struggle to really see its value.
mean, most people check their email the first thing they do in the morning.
So like, why wouldn't you want a newsletter?
I know, the signal's higher.
It is, you know, it's, it's an education.
I think what you'll find is that like the stakeholder that you're talking to is largely going to be the defining factor.
It's like, oh, is it a CMO that has done content work in the past and has experience with newsletters or whatever?
Or is it like a startup founder that doesn't know anything about content?
And in those cases, it's just like, hey, why don't you have a newsletter?
They're like, I don't know.
Is that important?
You're like, yes, it is.
Absolutely.
So I think that's part of it.
And we deal with all kinds of different ICPs or people just in different phases of their life as a company.
And maybe they have different kinds of experience on the team.
Some of them have really experienced CMOs.
Some of them don't.
And so sometimes it's like say the things out loud and act as the experts in a large number of cases.
They are not content experts.
And you just have to be the voice of reason and, you know, kind of like playing.
So we push back all the time.
It's useful, I think, and they value it.
People, they're literally just asking us for our opinion because they don't know.
So that's what we're there for.
But yeah, I know I do have to jump, but I just want to ask, did you articulate a little bit for me kind of some of the ways that you've used automation, either personally or on your job?
Kind of talk me through some of the processes that you've worked with, whether it's AI-related or other types of automation tooling.
Sure.
So I'm going to talk about two ways that I've used automation.
One was to create content library.
So at my last role, the deliverable that we would give to clients were usually like content library.
So like when you talk about like directories, glossary, sometimes people came to us to even write SMS messaging for their bots.
That's really what people came to us more for, but we started expanding.

Whenever we would use a prompt and create content, because we were also doing a lot of prompt work, that prompt output, we used, there's like a, if you, you know, Zapier integration, we connected it to GPT.

And GPT-4, because it was GPT-4 at the time, that output ends up in Google Sheets, which is already pre-prepped to fit the client's needs.
And then we hand over that content library as the Google Sheets structured data to the client.

And then from Notebook LLM, we would always sort of like interact with that repository to create cross-functional documentation.
